elements.sent-2411-001: Spin-polarized particle beams are of interest for applications like deep-inelastic scattering, e.g. to gain further understanding of the proton's nuclear structure.
elements.sent-2411-002: With the advent of high-intensity laser facilities, laser-plasma-based accelerators offer a promising alternative to common radiofrequency-based accelerators, as they can shorten the required acceleration length significantly.
elements.sent-2411-003: However, in the scope of spin-polarized particles, they bring unique challenges.
elements.sent-2411-004: This paper reviews the developments in the field of spin-polarized particles on the basis of the interaction of laser pulses and high-energy particle beams with plasma.
elements.sent-2411-005: The relevant scaling laws for spin-dependent effects in laser-plasma interaction, as well as acceleration schemes for polarized leptons, ions and gamma quanta are discussed.

elements.sent-2411-009: The study of spin dynamics in plasma has gained significant interest in recent years, specifically in the scope of generating relativistic, spin-polarized particle beams.
elements.sent-2411-010: Earlier research with polarized particle beams has, e.g., utilized the polarization build-up over time due to the Sokolov-Ternov effect in storage rings or used pre-polarized electron or hadron sources in the case of linear accelerators.
elements.sent-2411-011: A review of spin dynamics in ``conventional'' accelerators was given by Mane in \cite{Mane2005_review_spin .}
elements.sent-2411-012: However, the developments in high-intensity lasers and the progress of plasma-based accelerators have brought the option of obtaining and accelerating polarized particle beams via laser-plasma interaction within reach.
elements.sent-2411-013: Extensive reviews on the progress in the laser-driven wakefield acceleration of electrons and the different acceleration schemes for ions have been given by Esarey and Macchi et al., respectively \cite{esarey_LWFA_RMP, macchi_2013_RMP .}
elements.sent-2411-014: Reviews geared towards beam-driven wakefield accelerators were given by Hogan \cite{Hogan2016_pwfa_review , and Adli and Muggli \cite{Adli2016_pwfa_review in the specific case of a proton driver.}}
elements.sent-2411-015: For applications where polarization persistence is a requirement, the dynamics of particle spin have to be thoroughly understood.
elements.sent-2411-016: These applications, e.g., include the areas of high-energy physics and nuclear fusion.
elements.sent-2411-017: In the field of high-energy physics, polarized particle beams can be utilized for deep-inelastic scattering to improve the understanding of the proton nuclear structure \cite{Glashausser1979_nuclear and are of general relevance to quantum chromodynamics \cite{Gross2023_qcd .}}
elements.sent-2411-018: For nuclear fusion, Goldhaber et al. proposed in \cite{Goldhaber1934_fusion that the relevant cross-section depends on the relative spin orientation of the reactants.}
elements.sent-2411-019: For the reaction $d + t \to \alpha + n$, the enhancement factor is approximately 50\% \cite{Baylor2023_fusion .}
elements.sent-2411-020: For other reactions, like proton-Boron fusion, similar factors have been calculated \cite{Ahmed2013_pB_fusion .}
elements.sent-2411-021: Moreover, the understanding of spin effects in plasma has led to the idea of using polarization as a diagnostic tool for measuring the strength of electromagnetic fields in astrophysical plasma \cite{Gong2021_transient_fields, Gong2023_electron_filament .}
elements.sent-2411-022: This review provides an overview of the progress in the acceleration of spin-polarized particle beams via plasma-based acceleration schemes.
elements.sent-2411-023: The remainder of the introduction discusses the main spin-dependent effects that can become relevant during of high-intensity laser pulses or high-energy particle beams with plasma.
elements.sent-2411-024: Section \ref{sec:techniques describes some of the experimental techniques involved with spin, ranging from the production of polarized particle beams to their detection.}
elements.sent-2411-025: Results on polarized electrons and positrons are presented in section \ref{sec:leptons .}
elements.sent-2411-026: Similarly, theoretical and experimental results on polarized ions are presented in \ref{sec:ions .}
elements.sent-2411-027: The production of polarized gamma quanta is described in \ref{sec:gamma .}
elements.sent-2411-028: Lastly, a summary and a discussion of future prospects are found in section \ref{sec:summary .}

elements.sent-2411-032: Spin precession: The Thomas-Bargmann-Michel-Telegdi (T-BMT) equation \cite{Bargmann1959_tbmt, Thomas1926_tbmt describes spin precession according to the equation \begin{equation}\frac{\D \ssb}{\D t} = - \Omegab \times \ssb \; ,\end{equation} where \begin{equation}\Omegab = \frac{qe}{mc} \left[ \Omega_B \Bb - \Omega_v \left(\frac{\vb}{c} \cdot \Bb \right) \cdot \frac{\vb}{c} - \Omega_E \frac{\vb}{c} \times \Eb \right]\end{equation} is the precession frequency.}
elements.sent-2411-033: It depends on the prevalent electromagnetic fields $\Eb, \Bb$ and the particle's velocity $\vb$.
elements.sent-2411-034: Further, $qe$ denotes the charge of the particle, $m$ its mass and $c$ is the vacuum speed of light.
elements.sent-2411-035: The prefactors \begin{equation}\Omega_B = a + \frac{1}{\gamma} \; , \qquad \Omega_v = \frac{a \gamma}{\gamma + 1} \; , \qquad \Omega_E = a + \frac{1}{\gamma + 1} \; ,\end{equation} depend on the anomalous magnetic moment $a$ and the Lorentz factor $\gamma$ of the corresponding particle.
elements.sent-2411-036: The anomalous magnetic moment is defined as $a = (g-2)/2$ via the Land\'{e $g$-factor.}
elements.sent-2411-037: It can be calculated as higher-order loop corrections to the magnetic moment.
elements.sent-2411-038: For the electron it is $a_e \approx \alpha / (2\pi) \approx 10^{-3}$, for protons it is roughly $m_p / m_e$ times larger.
elements.sent-2411-039: Thomas et al. derived scaling laws for the precession frequency of electrons and protons in dependence of the dominant field strength $F = \mathrm{max (|\Eb|, |\Bb|)$.}
elements.sent-2411-040: In particular, they found the minimum depolarization time to be \begin{equation}T_{D,p} = \frac{\pi}{6.6 a_e F} \; .\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-041: Within the scope of their paper, the minimum depolarization time is defined as the characteristic time at which the maximum angle between the original direction of the particle spin and the final state is on the order of $\pi / 2$.
elements.sent-2411-042: As discussed later in more detail, particle beams preserve their polarization degree, as long as all particles are subject to the same homogeneous field.
elements.sent-2411-043: If locally varying fields like oscillating laser fields are applied, the polarization is quickly lost due to the differences in precession frequency.
elements.sent-2411-044: Spin-dependent trajectories: The most well-known example of spin-dependent trajectories is the Stern-Gerlach effect \cite{Gerlach1922_sg , in which particles carrying a magnetic moment $\bm{\mu}$ are subject to a gradient magnetic field.}
elements.sent-2411-045: The exerted force in that case is \begin{equation}\Fb = \nabla (\bm{\mu} \cdot \Bb) \; .\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-046: This description can be generalized to \begin{equation}\Fb_\mathrm{SG} = \left( \nabla - \frac{\D}{\D t} \nabla_\vb \right) (\Omegab \cdot \ssb) \; .\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-047: For particles in the GeV-range, Thomas et al. find the following scaling laws for the maximum particle separation of electrons and protons in terms of the laser wavelength $\lambda_L$ and the relativistic factor $\gamma$: \begin{eqnarray}\Delta_e (\partial F = 0) \propto 0.3 (2 + 3 a\gamma) \lambda_L [\mu\mathrm{m}]^{-1} \gamma^{-1} \; , \Delta_p (\partial F = 0) \propto 0.3 \left( \frac{m_e}{m}\right)^2 \lambda_L [\mu\mathrm{m}]^{-1} \gamma^{-1} \; .\end{eqnarray}.
elements.sent-2411-048: These scaling laws assume that the prevalent fields are somewhat homogeneous ($\partial F = 0$) like it would be the case in plasma channels.
elements.sent-2411-049: Radiative polarization: The Sokolov-Ternov effect describes the build-up of polarization due to spin flips during the emission of radiation \cite{Ternov1995_st .}
elements.sent-2411-050: As there is a slight difference in the probabilities for a flip from spin-up to spin-down compared to the opposite case, a particle beam can gain net polarization over time.
elements.sent-2411-051: This effect is commonly utilized in storage rings.
elements.sent-2411-052: The polarization of the beam builds up according to \begin{equation}P(t) = P_\mathrm{eq} [1 - \exp(-t/\tau_\mathrm{pol})] \; , \quad P_\mathrm{eq} = \frac{P_\uparrow - P_\downarrow}{P_\uparrow + P_\downarrow} \; ,\end{equation} where $P_\uparrow, P_\downarrow$ denote the different spin-flip probabilities.
elements.sent-2411-053: The scaling laws for the characteristic polarization time in terms of beam energy and field strength found by Thomas et al. are \begin{eqnarray}T_\mathrm{pol, electron} = \frac{10^{-7} \mathrm{s}}{T_e[\mathrm{GeV}]^2 F[\mathrm{TV/m}]^3} \; , T_\mathrm{pol, proton} = \frac{10^{14} \mathrm{s}}{T_p[\mathrm{GeV}]^2 F[\mathrm{TV/m}]^3} \; .\end{eqnarray}.
elements.formula-2411-009: \begin{equation}T_pol, electron = \frac{10^{-7} s}{T_e[GeV]^2 F[TV/m]^3} \; , T_pol, proton = \frac{10^{14} s}{T_p[GeV]^2 F[TV/m]^3} \; .\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-054: In their paper, Thomas et al. conclude that for most laser-plasma based accelerators (not including future high-intensity laser facilities), spin precession according to the T-BMT equation is the only effect of concern for polarization preservation, while the Stern-Gerlach force and the Sokolov-Ternov effect can mostly be neglected.
elements.sent-2411-055: More extensive discussions on radiative spin dynamics have been given by several authors in the framework of QED, like studies on spin-dependent radiation reaction by Seipt et al. \cite{Seipt_2019 .}
elements.sent-2411-056: Geng et al. identified a spin-dependent deflection mechanism based on radiation reaction that occurs during laser-electron interaction \cite{Geng2020_radiative_effect .}
elements.sent-2411-057: The effect is shown to dominate the Stern-Gerlach force for electrons with 100s of MeV and 10 PW-class laser pulses.

elements.sent-2411-063: In this section, we will cover aspects of targetry and polarimetry, in particular, how polarized targets for laser-plasma interaction may be produced and how the degree of polarization can be measured.
elements.sent-2411-064: For the experimental realization of polarized beam generation from laser-induced plasmas, the choice of the target is the most crucial point.
elements.sent-2411-065: Several concepts can be found in literature, split into (i) pre-polarized targets, where the spins of the particles to be accelerated are already aligned before the acceleration process sets in, and (ii) unpolarized targets, which require a spin-selective ionization or injection during the acceleration process.
elements.sent-2411-066: The only polarized target that has been used so far for laser-plasma acceleration experimentally employs a jet containing nuclear polarized $^3$He gas \cite{Fedorets2022_he3 .}
elements.sent-2411-067: This rare helium isotope has the advantage that it can be rather easily polarized at room temperature and be stored for many hours in a magnetic holding field (cf.. Fig. \ref{fig:fedorets_he3 ).}
elements.sent-2411-068: High degrees of nuclear polarization ($P\sim 40$\%) can be obtained in a gas jet at densities of a few times $10^{19}$\,cm$^{-3}$.
elements.sent-2411-069: The polarization for this and similar targets can be achieved via methods like metastability-exchange optical pumping \cite{Mrozik2011_meop or spin-exchange optical pumping \cite{Anderson2020_seop .}}
elements.sent-2411-070: Data of an experiment at the PHELIX laser suggest persistence of this polarization after acceleration of the $^3$He ions to MeV energies \cite{Zheng2023_evidence_he3 .}
elements.sent-2411-071: A disadvantage of pre-polarized targets, such as $^3$He gas, is that a permanent magnetic holding field must be provided around the interaction zone in order to maintain the target polarization.
elements.sent-2411-072: In the case of $^3$He gas, the field strength requirement is rather low, on the order of a few mT, but great care has to be taken on the homogeneity of the holding field \cite{Fedorets2022_he3 .}
elements.sent-2411-073: Such fields can either be provided by permanent-magnet assemblies or by a Helmholtz-type coils.
elements.sent-2411-074: These have the disadvantage of requiring cooling (if mounted inside the laser-interaction chamber) but allow the initial $^3\mkern-2mu$He polarization to be rotated relative to the laser propagation axis, thus providing maximum flexibility in adjusting the beam polarization.
elements.sent-2411-075: Gas targets containing high-density spin-polarized hydrogen (SPH) have been proposed for the acceleration of electrons (see Ref. \cite{Popp2021,sofikitis2024highenergypolarizedelectronbeams and references therein) as well as proton or deuteron beams (see for example Ref. \cite{Jin2020_mva ).}}
elements.sent-2411-076: The SPH is produced through the photo-dissociation of unpolarized hydrogen halides and contains --- dependent on the application --- either electrons or protons/deuterons with high degrees of polarization.
elements.sent-2411-077: The principle behind these targets is to first align the molecular bond of diatomic gas molecules in the electric field of a linearly polarized laser pulse.
elements.sent-2411-078: This is followed by the photo-dissociation into molecular states with polarized valence electrons using a second, circularly polarized UV laser pulse.
elements.sent-2411-079: The hyperfine structure of hydrogen atoms then causes the polarization to oscillate from the electron to the proton (deuteron) and backwards, with a period of 0.7\,ns.
elements.sent-2411-080: Finally, a third laser pulse drives the acceleration process.
elements.sent-2411-081: The advantage of these targets is that the polarized target material can be resupplied to the interaction zone at high rates, no magnetic holding fields are required and densities close to critical density can be achieved.
elements.sent-2411-082: While the use of SPH targets seems rather straightforward for proton acceleration \cite{Jin2020_mva,Huetzen2019_protons , the acceleration of polarized electrons would first require the removal of unpolarized electrons from the accelerating plasma which are unavoidably present in the (otherwise unused) halide atoms.}
elements.sent-2411-083: Two solutions have been put forward here, either removal via ionization \cite{Wu2019_lwfa or spatial separation of the SPH from the unwanted photoproducts leaving behind only hydrogen atoms of high polarization ($P = 90$\%) \cite{sofikitis2024highenergypolarizedelectronbeams .}}
elements.sent-2411-084: It remains to be seen which of these approaches will ultimately prove to be more practicable.

elements.sent-2411-086: According to Ref. \cite{Engels2020molecules , hyperpolarized cryogenic foils can be produced after the recombination of nuclear polarized hydrogen atoms from an atomic beam source to hydrogen-deuterium molecules.}

elements.sent-2411-100: In order to be experimentally accessible, the information about beam polarization has to be converted into measurable quantities.
elements.sent-2411-101: This is generally achieved by sampling the spin projection of a large number of particles in a secondary scattering process that is sensitive to the spin direction.
elements.sent-2411-102: Both the scattering target and the particle detectors are typically combined in a single device, the so-called polarimeter.
elements.sent-2411-103: Such polarimeters are frequently used at conventional particle accelerators to measure beam polarizations or polarization-dependent scattering cross sections.
elements.sent-2411-104: However, at laser-plasma accelerators, particular challenges have to be overcome, such as the time structure of the beams and strong electromagnetic pulses induced by the accelerating laser.
elements.sent-2411-105: This often prohibits the use of sophisticated electronic detectors to resolve individual beam particles.
elements.sent-2411-106: Since the scattering processes in polarimeters crucially depend on the particle species and energy these must be carefully customized for the respective application.
elements.sent-2411-107: The first polarimeter for laser-accelerated particles was used to measure the polarization of TNSA-accelerated, few-MeV protons at the 100 TW Arcturus laser facility, Düsseldorf \cite{Raab2014_pol_measurement .}
elements.sent-2411-108: The polarization measurement is based on the spin dependence of elastic proton scattering off nuclei in a silicon target.
elements.sent-2411-109: This scattering process has a non-vanishing analyzing power $A_y$ which is a measure for the asymmetry of the scattered protons' angular distribution along the azimuthal angle for a given transversal beam polarization.
elements.sent-2411-110: Silicon is well suited as scattering target material at proton energies of a few MeV since high precision data for $A_y$ are available from literature.
elements.sent-2411-111: The scattered protons were recorded behind the scattering target with CR-39 detectors for each individual laser shot.
elements.sent-2411-112: The authors of Ref. \cite{Raab2014_pol_measurement conclude that polarizations down to $P\sim20$\% can be detected for particle bunches with a sufficiently high number of produced protons ($\sim 10^8$ with energies above 2.5\,MeV).}
elements.sent-2411-113: A compact polarimeter for $^3$He ions with special emphasis on the analysis of short-pulsed beams accelerated during laser–plasma interactions is described in Ref. \cite{Zheng2022_polarimetry_he3 .}
elements.sent-2411-114: The polarimeter was tested at a tandem accelerator with unpolarized $^3$He beams and then used for the analysis of polarized laser-accelerated ions \cite{Zheng2023_evidence_he3 .}
elements.sent-2411-115: It is based on the detection of $\alpha$ particles in CR-39 stacks, emitted from the $d$-$^3$He fusion reaction with non-vanishing $A_y$ in a deuterated-polyethylene foil.
elements.sent-2411-116: The polarimeter is especially useful for the analysis of $^3$He ion beams in the few-MeV range, but also at lower energies down to 0.6 MeV.
elements.sent-2411-117: The accuracy of a beam polarization measurement, i.e., the minimal detectable $^3$He beam polarization, is estimated to be 21\% for a typical laser–plasma experiment where roughly $10^9$ $^3$He ions enter the polarimeter.
elements.sent-2411-118: An electron polarimeter for the LEAP project is being prepared at DESY, Hamburg \cite{Popp:2024Cz .}
elements.sent-2411-119: For their expected electron beam energies of tens of MeV, Compton transmission polarimetry is the ideal method to measure the polarization.
elements.sent-2411-120: The transmission asymmetry with respect to the magnetization direction is proportional to the initial electron polarization.
elements.sent-2411-121: Calibration measurements with unpolarized beams are in progress.
elements.sent-2411-122: At higher electron energies (100 MeV and above), the asymmetries for Compton transmission polarimetry decrease and this method becomes impractical.
elements.sent-2411-123: Mott-, M{\o ller- or laser-Compton-polarimetry must be used instead, however, such polarimeters have not yet been developed for laser-plasma applications.}

elements.sent-2411-558: Perhaps the most immediate application of laser-driven polarized particles is as injectors into conventional accelerator facilities.
elements.sent-2411-559: Conventional polarized sources, such as strained-GaAs photocathodes, are constrained to average currents in the milliampere regime.
elements.sent-2411-560: In contrast, laser-plasma injectors can deliver kiloampere-scale polarized bunches that can be captured and accelerated further in RF linacs or storage rings.
elements.sent-2411-561: This approach provides access to high-energy, high-brightness polarized beams well beyond the limits of conventional injectors, supporting future polarized electron-ion colliders (EICs) at higher instantaneous luminosity for spin-structure studies.. \ack Z.G.
